verses.text: The whole congregation of the children of Israel assembled together at Shiloh, and set up the tabernacle of the congregation there. And the land was subdued before them. Guidance: The tabernacle moves from Gilgal (where Israel was reborn) to Shiloh (where it will rest for 369 years). Shiloh — 'rest' or 'place of peace' — sits in Ephraim's hill country, near the geographic heart of the new nation. The text gives the only verb the chapter needs: 'the land was subdued.' Before more allocating, Israel re-centers itself around the LORD's dwelling. Worship before division. The tent comes down before the deeds go out.

There remained among the children of Israel seven tribes, which had not yet received their inheritance. Joshua said unto the children of Israel: How long are ye slack to go to possess the land, which the LORD God of your fathers hath given you? Guidance: Seven tribes have not even bothered to draw lots. Joshua, now elderly, asks a question that stings: 'How long are ye slack?' The land is given; they will not go take it. Sometimes the obstacle to inheritance is not Canaanite armies but Israelite inertia. God gives more than His people are eager to claim. A given gift is still received only when you step out to take it.

There remained among the children of Israel seven tribes, which had not yet received their inheritance. Sometimes the obstacle to inheritance is not Canaanite armies but Israelite inertia. God gives more than His people are eager to claim. A given gift is still received only when you step out to take it.

Give out from among you three men for each tribe: and I will send them, and they shall rise, and go through the land, and describe it according to the inheritance of them; and they shall come again to me. They shall divide it into seven parts: Judah shall abide in their coast on the south, and the house of Joseph shall abide in their coasts on the north. Ye shall therefore describe the land into seven parts, and bring the description hither to me, that I may cast lots for you here before the LORD our God. But the Levites have no part among you; for the priesthood of the LORD is their inheritance: and Gad, and Reuben, and half the tribe of Manasseh, have received their inheritance beyond Jordan on the east, which Moses the servant of the LORD gave them. Guidance: Twenty-one surveyors, three per tribe — a sober middle path between Joshua's appointment and the people's reluctance. He does the dividing; they do the walking. Faith is still partnership: God owns the land, leaders frame the lots, ordinary people pace the ground. The Levites' note returns again: priesthood is their inheritance. Three times Joshua records this — God's ministers do not need acreage when they have an altar.

The men arose, and went away: Joshua charged them that went to describe the land: Go walk through the land, describe it, come again to me, that I may here cast lots for you before the LORD in Shiloh. The men went and passed through the land, described it by cities into seven parts in a book, and came again to Joshua to the host at Shiloh. Joshua cast lots for them in Shiloh before the LORD: there Joshua divided the land unto the children of Israel according to their divisions. Guidance: Notice the verbs: 'go walk through, describe, bring back, lay before the LORD.' Surveying is the ordinary part; the casting of the lot is the holy part. Practical detail and prayer alternate without conflict. The result is recorded 'in a book' — Israel's first land registry. God is not allergic to paperwork; He uses it. The dignity of work is in offering its result to Him.

The lot of the tribe of the children of Benjamin came up according to their families: the coast of their lot came forth between the children of Judah and the children of Joseph. Their border on the north side was from Jordan; the border went up to the side of Jericho on the north, went up through the mountains westward; the goings out thereof were at the wilderness of Beth-aven. The border went over from thence toward Luz, to the side of Luz (which is Beth-el) southward; the border descended to Ataroth-adar, near the hill that lieth on the south side of the nether Beth-horon. The border was drawn thence, and compassed the corner of the sea southward, from the hill that lieth before Beth-horon southward; the goings out thereof were at Kirjath-baal, which is Kirjath-jearim, a city of the children of Judah: this was the west quarter. The south quarter was from the end of Kirjath-jearim, the border went out westward unto the fountain of the waters of Nephtoah: the border came down to the end of the mountain that lieth before the valley of the son of Hinnom, in the valley of the giants on the north, descended to the valley of Hinnom to the side of Jebusi on the south, descended to En-rogel; was drawn from the north, went forth to En-shemesh, went forth toward Geliloth, which is over against the going up of Adummim, descended to the stone of Bohan son of Reuben; passed toward the side over against Arabah northward, went down unto Arabah; passed along to the side of Beth-arabah northward, descended unto the plain; passed along to the side of Beth-hoglah northward: the outgoings of the border were at the north bay of the salt sea at the south end of Jordan: this was the south coast. Jordan was the border of it on the east side. This was the inheritance of the children of Benjamin, by the coasts thereof round about, according to their families. Guidance: Benjamin is wedged between his two famous brothers — Judah to the south, Joseph (Ephraim and Manasseh) to the north. The youngest son of Jacob (Gen 35:18) becomes the buckle that holds the central tribes together. Jerusalem (then called Jebusi) sits on Benjamin's southern border. The first king Saul will rise from this tribe, then Paul of Tarsus will rise from it as well — Benjamin produces both a king who fell and an apostle who rose. The smallest tribe holds the most strategic geography.

The first king Saul will rise from this tribe, then Paul of Tarsus will rise from it as well — Benjamin produces both a king who fell and an apostle who rose. The smallest tribe holds the most strategic geography.

The cities of the tribe of the children of Benjamin according to their families were Jericho, Beth-hoglah, the valley of Keziz, Beth-arabah, Zemaraim, Beth-el, Avim, Parah, Ophrah, Chephar-haammonai, Ophni, Gaba — twelve cities with their villages. Gibeon, Ramah, Beeroth, Mizpeh, Chephirah, Mozah, Rekem, Irpeel, Tarala, Zelah, Eleph, Jebusi (which is Jerusalem), Gibeath, Kirjath — fourteen cities with their villages. This is the inheritance of the children of Benjamin according to their families. Guidance: Twenty-six cities — small in number, heavy in destiny. Jericho (Israel's first conquest), Bethel (Jacob's ladder), Mizpeh (Samuel's revival site), Ramah (Samuel's home), Gibeon (the deceivers' city), Gibeath (Saul's birthplace), and Jebusi/Jerusalem (the future capital). Almost every story-defining location of early Israel sits in this small tribe's ledger. Faith does not need land area; it needs the right addresses. Benjamin's deed reads like a future table of contents.
